Why this exists

Neither engine behind this page was built for terrain. My geospatial platform was built for real-estate market data: county records ingested into a hashed, auditable ledger and served read-only. My geometry engine was built from first principles in a bare terminal with nothing but C++ and g++ installed (low-level predicates, triangulation, Voronoi, no libraries), to see whether I could. Neither had anything to do with mapping ground.

This page is what happened when I pointed them at real ground anyway. Public USGS elevation goes in; the geometry engine turns it into a triangulated surface; the earthwork and drainage are computed on that surface; a real lot boundary would come from the platform. Empty drawing board to a working, genuinely useful tool in about an hour and a half, and nearly all of it ran on my own machine on my own code (the public elevation data and the plotting aside). That speed isn't luck, and it isn't a one-off: it's the payoff of building each piece rigorously from the start, so two of them bolt into something new and hold. That's how I build and how I think, and it generalizes. This tool is one instance of it, not the exception.

Terrain & earthwork · demonstration

Reading a lot before you build

Two things the shape of the ground tells you before a shovel moves: how much dirt it takes to cut a level pad, and where the rain goes. Both are worked out on a model of the real ground: a triangulated surface my geometry engine builds from public USGS elevation.

Study 1 · Building padFoothill lot · Fresno County · 37.08°N 119.49°W

A sloping foothill lot. The question is earthwork: what does a level building pad cost in dirt?

39 ft
Fall across lot
12.9 %
Steepest grade
2,089 ft
Balanced pad
18,556 yd³
Earth moved
~0 yd³
Hauled off-site

The pad

You cut the high side down and fill the low side up until it's level. Put the pad at the balanced grade (2,089 ft) and the dirt you remove matches the dirt you need, so almost nothing is trucked in or hauled off. On a slope, that difference is most of the grading bill.

The dirt

Making the pad moves about 18,556 cubic yards (roughly 9,300 out, 9,300 back in), so off-site haul is essentially nothing. Every number traces to a measured point on the ground.

The water

The lot drains southwest and collects at the low corner. Put a swale or drain there before you pour; it's cheaper than fixing it after the first storm.

Study 2 · RainwaterWest bluff · San Joaquin River rim · north Fresno

A harder question, on much steeper ground: where does the rain go? This is the west bluff behind the rim lots, where the ground falls 95 feet to the San Joaquin.

95 ft
Fall down bluff
39 %
Steepest grade
NW
Bluff faces
604k gal
Runoff · 1-in storm · 22 ac
WNW
Drains to river

The slope

A steep west-northwest-facing bluff dropping 95 feet toward the San Joaquin, 39% at the steepest.

The runoff

There's no holding this water in place; it runs off the face. A 1-inch storm sheds roughly 604,000 gallons off the modeled slope, all of it running downhill.

Where it goes

The runoff gathers into a draw that cuts northwest down the bluff and exits toward the river. Build near the rim and that draw is the line to respect: it's where the water and the erosion go.

How this was made

01 · elevation

Real USGS 10-meter elevation, sampled across each site: 100 points on the pad, 576 on the bluff.

02 · surface

The points are triangulated into a continuous surface (a TIN) by a C++ geometry engine I built from scratch. 162 and 1,058 triangles, exact to Euler's formula.

03 · analysis

From that surface: cut and fill balanced to a pad, and rainfall routed downhill neighbor-to-neighbor to find where water concentrates.

County GIS · layer ready

It runs on the same engine as my county GIS platform, so a lot boundary can come from official parcel data, and either study runs on any parcel in the county from its address alone.

A demonstration of the method on real public data, not a licensed survey or construction document. Locations are approximate; satellite imagery courtesy Esri World Imagery. Ten-meter data suits planning and early pricing, and the same pipeline reads a surveyed point cloud without change.
